revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祇園・長須賀古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Kinreizuka-1.jpg|location={{ill|Kisarazu|qid=Q335427|1=Kisarazu}}}}
'''Gion-Nagasuka Kofun Cluster''' (祇園・長須賀古墳群) refers to a group of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} (ancient Kofuns) located in {{ill|Kisarazu|en|Kisarazu|qid=Q335427|lt=Kisarazu|1=Kisarazu}}, Chiba Prefecture. These Kofuns were constructed from the mid-{{ill|5th century|en|5th century|qid=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}} to the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|qid=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}, primarily on the alluvial plains near the lower reaches of the {{ill|Obitsu River|en|Obitsu River|qid=Q7074982|lt=Obitsu River|1=Obitsu River}}.
== Overview == The Gion-Nagasuka Kofun Cluster was built on the low elevated areas that were once sand dunes in the alluvial plains formed by the Obitsu River. Four rows of north-south sand dune traces have been identified in the area, and kofun were primarily constructed along the second, third, and fourth rows from the coast. Additionally, some kofun were built near the hill area where {{ill|Ōta-yama Park|ja|太田山公園|1=Ōdayama Park|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-yama Park|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdayama Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q575332}} is located. The cluster is attributed to the {{ill|Makuta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|馬来田国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makuta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|1=Makuta Kuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11667981}}, the regional rulers of the Obitsu River basin during this period<ref>Ozawa (2007), p.140; Shirai (2009), pp.21–24</ref>.
Urbanization of the Kisarazu area led to significant alteration or destruction of many kofun in the cluster. Some, like the {{ill|Kinreizuka Kofun|ja|金鈴塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinreizuka Kofun|qq=}}, have survived only in a heavily altered state. Consequently, the full extent of the cluster is challenging to determine today.
== Naming == The cluster is also referred to as the "Kisarazu Kofun Cluster" or "Gion Kofun Cluster." Researchers proposed the name "Gion-Nagasuka Kofun Cluster" to specifically denote the kofun centered in the Gion and Nagasuka districts of Kisarazu, which has since gained wider acceptance<ref>Shiraishi (2007), p.116; Ozawa (2008), pp.368–369; Shirai (2009), p.21</ref>.
== History of the Kofun Cluster == === Origin === Construction of kofun in the Obitsu River basin dates back to the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} in the {{ill|4th century|en|4th century|qid=Q8099|lt=4th century|1=4th century}}. Large keyhole-shaped kofun such as the {{ill|Iitsubo Kofun|ja|飯籠塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitsubo Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504332|1=Iitsubo Kofun}}, with a length of approximately 100 meters, were constructed in the midstream region, while smaller kofun were built near the river’s mouth. The size of these kofun indicates that the regional rulers of the Obitsu River basin were already powerful by this time<ref>Ozawa (2008), pp.39–42; p.178</ref>.
The earliest kofun in the Gion-Nagasuka Cluster is believed to be the {{ill|Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|高柳銚子塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun|qq=}}, constructed on the second row of sand dunes. It is estimated to have been built in the second quarter of the 5th century, with a mound length of 130–150 meters. This kofun's construction indicates a shift of power within the Obitsu River basin<ref>Ozawa (2008), p.283</ref>.
Subsequently, other kofun such as the {{ill|Gion Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|祇園大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}}, with an estimated length of 100 meters, were built near the fourth row of sand dunes<ref>Ozawa (2008), p.178</ref>.
=== Resumption and Peak Construction === After a hiatus during the late 5th century, kofun construction resumed around the mid-6th century with the {{ill|Sakemorizuka Kofun|ja|酒盛塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakemorizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504336|1=Sakemorizuka Kofun}}, estimated at 90–100 meters in length. The 6th century saw the construction of increasingly elaborate kofun, culminating in the {{ill|Kinreizuka Kofun|ja|金鈴塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinreizuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Maruyama Kofun|ja|丸山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama Kofun|qq=}}. Kinreizuka Kofun is noted for its unplundered stone chamber, which contained numerous luxury items, including gold-plated swords and decorative shoes, underscoring the immense power of the buried individual<ref name="#1">Wada (2007), pp.45–46; Ota (2007), pp.97–101</ref>.
=== Transition to Square Tombs === Following the construction of the last keyhole-shaped kofun in the early 7th century, square kofun like the {{ill|Matsumoto Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} began to appear. The Matsumoto Kofun, with a side length of approximately 44 meters, is notable for its double moats and extensive burials<ref>Ozawa (2008), p.437; Shirai (2009), pp.22–24</ref>.
== Key Kofun == * {{ill|Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|高柳銚子塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun|qq=}} – Estimated length: 130–150 meters. * {{ill|Gion Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|祇園大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} – Estimated length: 100 meters. * {{ill|Kinreizuka Kofun|ja|金鈴塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinreizuka Kofun|qq=}} – Estimated length: 100 meters. * {{ill|Maruyama Kofun|ja|丸山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama Kofun|qq=}} – Estimated length: 70 meters. * {{ill|Matsumoto Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} – Square-shaped, with a side length of approximately 44 meters.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Kisarazu City Board of Education. *Kisarazu City Cultural Properties Research Collection 11*. 2006. * {{ill|Shiraishi Taichiro|ja|白石太一郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraishi Taichirō|qq=}}. *Ancient Tombs and Ancient History of the Eastern Provinces*. Gakuseisha, 2007. ISBN 978-4-311-20298-8. * Hiroyuki Ota. *Late Kofun Groups in Northern Musashi*. Rokuchi Shobō, 2007. ISBN 978-4-947743-55-8. * Hiroshi Ozawa. *Research on Kofun Culture in Bōsō*. Rokuchi Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-947743-69-5. * Takehiko Yoshimura and Naomitsu Yamaji, eds. *Bōsō and Ancient Authority*. Kōshi Shoin, 2009. ISBN 978-4-86215-054-7.
== External Links == * [http://arcadia.archaeo.cs.titech.ac.jp/documents/detail/6351 Gion-Nagasuka Kofun Cluster] * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/makuta.html Exploring Bōsō's Kofun: The Makuta Domain's Tombs]
